stewart_read: William's 'Say what?' is the earliest pace signal in the call. He needs a beat to orient — this is not hostility, it is a processing gap. Ella's response is to repeat the name question at the same pace without adjustment. The codex's energy_match principle (target pace 140-170 WPM, longer pause tolerance for the 45-70 demographic) applies here. This signal connects directly to the 02:09 interruption: Ella never adjusted her pace after the first signal, and the pattern compounds.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have simply not registered 'Say what?' as a pace signal and treated it as a mishear.
coaching_implication: Show Ella the 00:05 moment and the 02:09 interruption together. The question for the 1-on-1: 'What did you hear at 00:05, and what did you do differently after it?' If the answer is 'nothing,' that is the coaching gap — pace calibration on the first signal, not after the third.

quote: It's called what? No. Slow down. It's called what?
stewart_read: William explicitly asks Ella to slow down mid-pitch. This is a direct pace complaint, not an objection to solar. Ella's response is to repeat the program name and continue the pitch at the same rate. The 00:05 'Say what?' was the first signal; this is the second. By this point the rep has two explicit pace signals and has adjusted for neither. The bill swap program introduction is also the first time Ella introduces a named program — the customer's confusion ('It's called what?') may also reflect that the program name landed as jargon before any value framing.
coaching_implication: This is a pace and framing gap. In the 1-on-1, play 02:09 and ask: 'What did William tell you here, and what did you do with it?' The coaching move is not to slow down after being asked — it is to have already adjusted after 00:05. The program name introduction also warrants review: 'bill swap program' as a label before any value context is set up to confuse.

quote: Yes. I just need to check it. My name has been, run through, a bunch of fraudulent activities.
stewart_read: Ella cuts William off mid-sentence at 03:28 — he is in the middle of explaining 'Yes. I just need to check it. My name has been...' and Ella interrupts to redirect to Credit Karma. This is an active listening failure layered on top of the empathy miss. William is not resisting the credit check; he is trying to explain why the number will not reflect his actual creditworthiness. The interruption signals that Ella is tracking the script, not the customer.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have interpreted William's pause as a completed thought and moved to fill the silence — a pacing error rather than a deliberate cut.
coaching_implication: Play 03:28 in the 1-on-1 and ask Ella what William was about to say. The coaching point is not 'don't interrupt' in the abstract — it is that the sentence William was building ('my name has been run through fraudulent activities') was the information Ella needed to route this call differently. The interruption prevented her from hearing the full context.

quote: I'm 81 years old. These assholes are about 20, 30, 40 years old during the, COVID nineteen epidemic. People were just — they took over my house while I was in the VA hospital for three months.
stewart_read: This is the call's most significant moment. William discloses age (81), military service (VA hospital), COVID-era exploitation, and home invasion while hospitalized. The codex classifies this as a trust gift — a vulnerability disclosure that requires acknowledgment before any procedural move. Ella's response is 'Alright. Sounds good.' — a filler phrase that signals she is not tracking the content. The strategic-vs-reactive question here: was Ella deferring acknowledgment to stay on track? No — she closes the call 40 seconds later without any acknowledgment. This is script-lock, not strategy. [Note: Ion-specific phrasing for this vulnerability category is TBD pending Kenny's input per coaching_philosophy.empathy_on_vulnerability_disclosure. The direction is: acknowledge specifically, bridge to how solar helps this situation. 15 seconds of acknowledgment here would have changed the close of this call — at minimum, William would have felt heard before the credit conversation resumed.]
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have been trained to stay on the credit qualifier and not engage with personal disclosures, treating them as off-topic.
coaching_implication: This is the priority coaching moment for this call. Play 04:13 and ask Ella: 'What did William tell you here?' Then ask: 'What did you say back?' The gap between those two answers is the coaching. The codex is explicit: vulnerability disclosure is a trust gift, not an interruption. A veteran, 81 years old, describing COVID-era exploitation and VA hospitalization is not a credit-score conversation — it is a human moment that required a human response before any procedural move.

quote: I know all that's required to qualify is just have a credit score above 650. Is that the case for you?
stewart_read: Ella states the credit threshold as 650. The codex schema records 670 as the threshold, with a noted drift in SESSION18_fcbf33c1 where a rep also stated 650. This is the second instance of the 650 figure appearing on the floor. Either the active threshold is 650 (and the schema needs updating) or reps are consistently understating the threshold. This is a schema reconciliation question, not a coaching failure on Ella specifically — but it needs resolution before Stewart can grade credit-qualifier moments accurately.
alternative_hypothesis: The threshold may have been updated to 650 at the floor level without the schema being revised.
coaching_implication: Flag for manager to confirm the active credit threshold with Kenny. This is the second call where 650 appears (SESSION18_fcbf33c1 is the first). If the threshold is 670, both reps are understating it. If it is 650, the codex needs correction. Do not coach Ella on this until the threshold is confirmed.

quote: half of the roof hasn't been done probably for twenty years.
stewart_read: William discloses that half his roof is approximately 20 years old — a hard DQ condition per dq_rules (roof_too_old_no_replacement). The codex exception is explicit: if the customer wants roof replacement, route to the roofing team. Ella acknowledges the roof age ('Okay. So the back half of the roof is six years old.') but does not probe whether William has plans to replace the older half, and does not bridge to Ion's roofing team. The call then moves to the credit qualifier without resolving the roof DQ. This is both a cross-sell miss and a protocol gap — the roof condition warranted a direct question ('Have you thought about replacing that older section?') before moving forward.
alternative_hypothesis: Ella may have assessed that the six-year-old back half was sufficient for a solar install and deprioritized the older front half — but the codex threshold is 15 years, and 20 years exceeds it.
coaching_implication: Show Ella the dq_rules entry and the cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned section together. The question she needed to ask at 01:50: 'Have you thought about replacing that older section?' If yes, that is a roofing team referral. If no, that is a DQ conversation. Either way, the call needed to resolve the roof condition before moving to credit.

quote: Do you understand what I'm saying?
stewart_read: William's direct question — 'Do you understand what I'm saying?' — is the clearest signal in the call that he felt unheard. It is an invitation to repair. Ella's response ('Yes. I do understand.') states understanding without demonstrating it, then immediately repeats the 650 threshold and closes the call. The repair opportunity was available and unused. The strategic-vs-reactive question: was Ella setting up a callback by closing cleanly? No — she provides no callback mechanism, no name, no number, no timeframe. The close is a procedural exit, not a strategic one.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have interpreted 'Do you understand what I'm saying?' as a rhetorical close rather than a genuine request for acknowledgment.
coaching_implication: Play 05:22 and ask Ella: 'What was William asking you here?' Then play her response. The coaching point: 'Yes. I do understand.' followed immediately by the threshold repetition is the definition of stated-but-not-demonstrated empathy. The repair move available here was to reflect back what William said before any procedural close — not to agree and move on.

quote: Alright. Sounds good. Well, once you're able to figure out that credit score, just get back to us, and we can help out with this lower estimate. Okay?
stewart_read: Ella's instruction to William — 'just get back to us' — is a soft drop with no structure. There is no callback time, no direct contact information, no name to ask for, and no acknowledgment that William's credit situation is fraud-related rather than dispositional. The codex's spencer_operator_note on credit qualification is directly relevant: a customer who self-reports uncertain credit due to documented identity theft is not the same as a customer with genuinely poor credit. Ella has no routing for this distinction and defaults to 'call us back when you know.' The outcome is a lead that will almost certainly not re-engage — William has no mechanism to follow up and no reason to believe the follow-up will go differently.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have been following a floor protocol of not booking appointments until credit is confirmed, making the 'call back' instruction procedurally correct even if emotionally mishandled.
coaching_implication: The manager should surface the spencer_operator_note from qualifiers.credit_qualifier in the 1-on-1: stated credit is not pulled credit, and fraud-damaged credit is a different category entirely. The coaching question for Ella: 'What would you do differently if a customer told you their credit was damaged by identity theft, not by their own choices?' The floor may need a routing path for this scenario — flag for Kenny.
